perturbation.original_text: In the event that any Party takes legal action as a result of a breach of this Agreement, the non -breaching Party shall be entitled to recover reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred in connection with such legal action in the event that it prevails in one or more of its claims.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that any Party takes legal action as a result of a breach of this Agreement, each party shall bear its own legal fees and expenses, regardless of the outcome of such legal action.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the entitlement of the non-breaching party to recover legal fees and expenses if they prevail, potentially violating laws that allow for the recovery of such fees in contract disputes, especially where a breach involves bad faith or wilful misconduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 98 of the Civil Procedure Act 2005 (NSW) grants the court discretion to award costs, including legal fees, based on the circumstances of the case.  While the Act does not mandate awarding costs to the prevailing party, contract clauses that completely eliminate the possibility of cost recovery, regardless of fault or conduct, may be challenged, especially if one party acted in bad faith. Removing the clause that allows the non-breaching party to recover legal fees contradicts the general principle that the court has the discretion to allocate costs based on fairness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: View - NSW legislation Skip to main content Site header Search Home About Contact Help Links Export Feedback Site navigation A Table Of Contents View whole Act Statutory instruments Turn history notes on Legislative history Search Act Results: match 0 of 0 provisions Previous Hit Next Hit Return to search results Clear search 0 hits in page: First Last Civil Procedure Act 2005 No 28
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the non-breaching party to recover legal fees if they win. The changed text states that each party bears its own legal fees regardless of the outcome. The Law Explanation points out that Section 98 of the Civil Procedure Act 2005 (NSW) gives the court discretion to award costs, including legal fees. Removing the possibility of cost recovery contradicts the court's discretionary power and the principle of fairness in allocating costs. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the Civil Procedure Act 2005 No 28 as a relevant legal document.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information includes all information owned by a third party and disclosed by a Disclosing Party hereunder .
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information includes information disclosed by a Disclosing Party hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'all information owned by a third party' the contract fails to acknowledge the responsibility to protect 3rd party confidential information, which can result in legal action and regulatory fines.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) govern the handling of personal information. APP 1.1 requires organizations to manage personal information in an open and transparent way. By excluding third-party information from the definition of confidential information, the agreement may not adequately protect personal information disclosed by a third party. This change contradicts APP 1.1 as it creates a risk of failing to properly manage and protect third-party personal information, potentially leading to breaches of the Privacy Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Australian Privacy Principles | OAIC We use cookies on this site We use cookies to analyse traffic and to improve your browsing experience on our website. To find out more, read our privacy policy . Close Skip to main content Australian Privacy Principles The Australian Privacy Principles (or APPs) are the cornerstone of the privacy protection framework in the Privacy Act 1988 . They apply to any organisation or agency the Privacy Act covers. There are 13 Australian Privacy Principles and they govern standards, rights and obligations around: the collection, use and disclosure of personal information an organisation or agency’s governance and accountability integrity and correction of personal information the rights of individuals to access their personal information. The Australian Privacy Principles are principles-based law. This gives an organisation or agency flexibility to tailor their personal information handling practices to their business models and the diverse needs of individuals. They are also technology neutral, which allows them to adapt to changing technologies. A breach of an Australian Privacy Principle is an ‘interference with the privacy of an individual’ and can lead to regulatory action and penalties. perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the phrase "all information owned by a third party," which removes a crucial aspect of confidential information protection. The law explanation states that this change might not adequately protect personal information disclosed by a third party under the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs), potentially leading to breaches of the Privacy Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, "A breach of an Australian Privacy Principle is an ‘interference with the privacy of an individual’ and can lead to regulatory action and penalties." This supports the conclusion that omitting the third-party information clause contradicts the APPs by creating a risk of failing to manage and protect third-party personal information, leading to potential regulatory action.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt measures to protect the confidentiality, limited use and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information at least as restrictive as those it adopts for its own confidential information of similar importance and in any event, no less than reasonable;
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to adopt measures to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The contract weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text requires a level of protection *at least as restrictive* as the receiving party uses for its own confidential information, and in any event, *no less than reasonable*. Removing this creates an opportunity for the Receiving Party to provide inadequate protection, potentially violating legal obligations to safeguard trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New South Wales Crimes Act 1900 s 308C
perturbation.law_citation: New South Wales Crimes Act 1900 s 308C
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 308C of the Crimes Act 1900 (NSW) deals with the offense of dishonestly obtaining or disclosing commercial information. While this section primarily targets intentional acts, the failure to implement reasonable security measures, as originally stipulated in the NDA, could be seen as contributing to a breach of this section if commercially sensitive information is leaked due to negligence. By removing the 'at least as restrictive as its own' and 'no less than reasonable' clauses, the agreement becomes less protective, potentially increasing the risk of violating s 308C if a negligent act leads to the disclosure of confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CRIMES ACT 1900 - SECT 308C Unauthorised access, modification or impairment with intent to commit serious indictable offence All Databases Cases & Legislation Journals & Scholarship Law Reform Treaties Libraries Communities LawCite Australia CTH ACT NSW NT QLD SA TAS VIC WA New Zealand Specific Year Any 2015 Any Any CRIMES ACT 1900 - SECT 308C Unauthorised access, modification or impairment with intent to commit serious indictable offence CRIMES ACT 1900 - SECT 308C Unauthorised access, modification or impairment with intent to commit serious indictable offence 308C Unauthorised access , modification or impairment with intent to commit serious indictable offence (1) A person who causes any unauthorised computer function -- (a) knowing it is unauthorised , and (b) with the intention of committing a serious indictable offence , or facilitating the commission of a serious indictable offence (whether by the person or by another person), is guilty of an offence. : Maximum penalty--The maximum penalty applicable if the person had committed, or facilitated the commission of, the serious indictable offence in this jurisdiction. (2) For the purposes of this section, an "unauthorised computer function" is-- (a) any unauthorised access to data held in any computer, or (b) any unauthorised modification of data held in any computer, or (c) any unauthorised impairment of electronic communication to or from any computer. (3) For the purposes of this section, a "serious indictable offence" includes an offence in any other jurisdiction that would be a serious indictable offence if committed in this jurisdiction. (4) A person may be found guilty of an offence against this section-- (a) even if committing the serious indictable offence concerned is impossible, or (b) whether the serious indictable offence is to be committed at the time of the unauthorised conduct or at a later time. (5) It is not an offence to attempt to commit an offence against this section. Navigate Next Section Previous Section
perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates a level of confidentiality protection that is 'at least as restrictive' as the Receiving Party's own confidential information, but 'no less than reasonable'. The modified text removes these specific standards, potentially allowing for a lower level of protection than legally required. The Law Explanation suggests that removing the 'at least as restrictive as its own' and 'no less than reasonable' clauses weakens the agreement and could increase the risk of violating s 308C of the Crimes Act 1900 (NSW) if a negligent act leads to the disclosure of confidential information. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to 'Unauthorised access, modification or impairment with intent to commit serious indictable offence,' the Perturbation Explanation makes it clear that the omission creates a loophole, that makes the receiving party potentially negligent. This supports the assessment that omitting these provisions creates a legally questionable situation, since the receiving party is no longer required to adhere to an objective standard of care.

perturbation.original_text: A receiving Party shall return or destroy all copies of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information to the Disclo sing Party within ten (10) days after the Disclosing Party’s request.
perturbation.changed_text: A receiving Party shall make reasonable efforts to return or destroy all copies of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information after the Disclosing Party’s request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the obligation from a firm requirement to 'reasonable efforts' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information after the agreement ends. This may not meet the legal requirements for data destruction or return, especially concerning personal data.
perturbation.law_explanation: APP 11.2 of the Privacy Act 1988 requires an organisation to take reasonable steps to destroy or de-identify personal information if it is no longer needed for any purpose for which the information may be used or disclosed under the APPs. Replacing the original text with 'reasonable efforts' weakens the obligation and may not meet the requirement to take reasonable steps to destroy the information. The modification contradicts the legislation by providing a less stringent requirement for the destruction of confidential information and can cause legal issues when handling personal information.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the return or destruction of confidential information within ten days. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'reasonable efforts'. The Law Explanation states that APP 11.2 of the Privacy Act 1988 requires taking reasonable steps to destroy or de-identify personal information, suggesting a link between 'reasonable steps' and legal requirements. While Snippet 1 is a generic error message, the Perturbation and Law explanations together highlight that changing a firm obligation to 'reasonable efforts' may not satisfy the legal requirement for data destruction, especially regarding personal data, thus creating a legal contradiction as the modified clause could fail legal scrutiny.
